On November 21, 1935, the first conferral of the title of Marshal of the Soviet Union took place. Five military leaders received this title: Kliment Voroshilov, Alexander Yegorov, Vasily Blyukher, Semyon Budyonny, and Mikhail Tukhachevsky.

Yefim Ilyich Osipenko (January 7, 1902 – 1981) was a Soviet partisan who fought in the Civil War and the Great Patriotic War. He was a miner and later became the commander of a mobile group of the "Peredovoy" partisan detachment, operating in the Tula region. Near Myshbor station, after the fuse of a homemade mine failed, Osipenko detonated the charge by striking the detonator with a pole from a railway sign. As a result, a train carrying three flatcars loaded with tanks was derailed; the partisan survived but completely blinded. For his heroic deed, he became the first person in the Soviet Union to be awarded the medal "To a Partisan of the Patriotic War."

Maria Dmitrievna Vlaykova, born in 1917, was a Soviet military doctor and surgeon of the 153rd Medical-Sanitary Battalion, 192nd Rifle Division.

On July 24, 1942, near Stalingrad, German tanks and infantry broke through to the area of her medical battalion. As wounded soldiers lay in the tents, the doctors and cadets took up defensive positions.

Vlaykova was hit by three bullets and miraculously survived. She later received the Medal “For the Defence of Stalingrad,” the Order of the Red Star, and the Order of the Patriotic War, 2nd class.

After the war, she returned to Kaluga and continued working as an oncologist.

A Little Doll (Kukolka) is a 1988 Soviet psychological drama film directed by Isaak Fridberg.
The film tells about a young gymnast who is seriously injured, as a result of which she decides to leave the sport. But it’s very difficult for her to come to terms with the idea that she is now just like everyone else

Khanpasha Nuradilovich Nuradilov was a Soviet Chechen machine gunner in the Red Army between 1940 and 1942. According to Soviet records, he killed an estimated 920 enemy soldiers with his PM M1910 machine gun, making him one of the most celebrated machine gunners of World War II. He was also credited with capturing 18 German soldiers alive and seizing 7 enemy machine guns. Nuradilov died during the Battle of Stalingrad on September 12, 1942, and was posthumously awarded the title Hero of the Soviet Union.

The Tomb of the Unknown Soldier commemorates Georgian soldiers who served in the Red Army during the Great Patriotic War, many of whom lost their lives. The monument was officially opened in 1981 in Tbilisi’s Vake Park by Soviet General Secretary Leonid Brezhnev and First Secretary Eduard Shevardnadze, marking the 60th anniversary of the Georgian SSR.
